---
title: "DH Preliminary Model Diagnostics — post-2000 subset"
subtitle: "Family / exposure_mode decisions from the 2026-05-17 evaluate run (20260517)"
author: "Bobby Reiner"
date: "2026-05-17"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-location: left
    fig-width: 12
    fig-height: 5
    number-sections: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

## Scope

This report records the screening decisions made on the 2026-05-17 preliminary
double-hurdle (DH) evaluate run, refit on the **post-2000 subset** of the
EM-DAT cyclone mortality data (1,693 storm × admin rows, 2000–2021). The
all-years cycle (2026-05-07) is preserved as the audit record at
`reports/preliminary_decisions.qmd`; this fork records the decisions made on
the post-2000 data with two methodological changes.

**Why this cycle exists.** EM-DAT documents incomplete pre-2000 death
reporting; reporting completeness is plausibly SDI-correlated, which would
bias the SDI-mortality relationship — the suspected mechanistic cause of the
~30× low-SDI overprediction surfaced in 2026-05-16 predict-stage diagnostics.
Refitting on data with consistent reporting removes that bias source.

**The job remains the same:** discard families and exposure modes that
clearly cannot win, so that the downstream selection step works against a
narrower, more defensible grid. Five decision axes per stage: **family**,
**exposure_mode**, **covariate set**, **threshold_quantile**, and
(implicitly) the data / fold structure. We work through them stage by stage:

1. **S1** — `P(deaths >= 1)` — threshold-independent.
2. **S2** — `P(rate >= threshold | deaths >= 1)` — threshold-dependent.
3. **Bulk** — `E[rate | deaths >= 1, rate < threshold]` — threshold-dependent.
4. **Tail** — `E[rate | deaths >= 1, rate >= threshold]` — threshold-dependent.
5. **Threshold** — **deferred** to the intermediate-survivors analysis once
   per-storm predictions are loaded. The threshold question is best answered
   on calibration metrics that aren't dominated by 1-2 mega-events; that
   needs storm-level rather than config-level data.

### Calibration gate — asymmetric, not symmetric

The all-years cycle used `full_pred_obs_ratio_oos ∈ [0.9, 1.1]`. This cycle
**relaxes** the gate to `[0.1, 1.5]` for physically defensible reasons:

The death distribution in this dataset is dominated by a handful of mega-
events (Nargis 2008 alone is ~138K of the ~210K total deaths; the next few
are Haiyan 2013, Sidr 2007). A well-specified mortality model with a
threshold-aware tail will *honestly under-predict* those mega-events — they
live in the extreme upper tail and a 22-year window doesn't pin the mean
down. So `total_pred / total_obs < 1` is the **expected, correct** result.

A ratio of exactly 1.0 implies the model has *compensated* by
over-predicting the bulk-of-storms to cover the under-predicted mega-events.
That's worse than honest under-prediction — it's systematic per-storm bias
that happens to average to zero. The old `[0.9, 1.1]` gate was selecting
*for* this failure mode.

The new gate is asymmetric: **under-prediction up to 10× is allowed** (lower
bound 0.1, reflects mega-event honesty), but **over-prediction is capped at
1.5×** (compensating-overprediction failure mode). Per-stage heatmaps below
operate on the configs that pass this asymmetric gate.

### Time-series calibration metrics — computed, but not gates

Three new metrics are computed alongside the existing per-stage metrics:
`cor_ts_oos` (year-aggregate correlation between observed and predicted
deaths), `beta_p_ts_oos` (OLS slope), `beta_0_ts_oos` (OLS intercept). The
intent was to use them as screening gates. On this dataset they aren't
usable as gates: year-to-year variability in deaths is dominated by *which
years got a mega-event*, which is not encoded in the storm-level features
the model has access to. The achievable `cor_ts_oos` ceiling across all
configs is ~0.44 — a property of the data/model-family match, not of any
specific config's quality. The metrics appear in the survivors notebook as
horror-spotters, not as filters.

Each section below shows the one or two plots that drove the call, the
observations they support, and the locked-in decision.

```{python}
#| label: setup
import sys
from pathlib import Path

# Make the local helper module importable regardless of where Quarto runs from.
sys.path.insert(0, str(Path(__file__).resolve().parent if "__file__" in dir() else Path(".").resolve()))
import _helpers as H

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

ROOT      = Path("/mnt/team/idd/pub/idd_tc_mortality")
EVAL_DATE = "20260517"
RESULTS_PATH = ROOT / "02-evaluate" / EVAL_DATE / "dh_results.parquet"

df_all = H.load_and_aggregate(RESULTS_PATH)

# Asymmetric calibration gate: under-prediction (mega-event honesty) allowed
# down to 10× under; over-prediction (compensating bulk overprediction) capped
# tighter at 1.5×. See §Scope for the physics rationale.
RATIO_BOUNDS = (0.1, 1.5)
ratio      = df_all["full_pred_obs_ratio_oos"]
calibrated = ratio.between(*RATIO_BOUNDS) & ratio.notna()
df = df_all[calibrated].reset_index(drop=True)
print(f"{len(df):,} configs ({len(df)/len(df_all):.1%}) pass "
      f"full_pred_obs_ratio_oos in [{RATIO_BOUNDS[0]}, {RATIO_BOUNDS[1]}]")

# Per-stage deduplicated frames (calibrated subset).
s1_df   = H.dedup_for_stage(df, "s1")
s2_df   = H.dedup_for_stage(df, "s2")
bulk_df = H.dedup_for_stage(df, "bulk")
tail_df = H.dedup_for_stage(df, "tail")
```

## S1 — `P(deaths >= 1)`

S1 is fit on the full dataset. Its outcome (`any_death`) does not depend on
`threshold_quantile`, so the same `(s1_family, s1_exposure_mode, s1_cov)`
spec produces identical metrics across every DH configuration that uses it.
Two structural choices to make:

- **family**: `logit` or `cloglog`.
- **exposure_mode**: how `log_exposed` enters the linear predictor.
  - `excluded` — drop log_exposed.
  - `free`     — include with a freely estimated coefficient.
  - `offset`   — include with coefficient locked at 1 (cloglog only;
    corresponds to the proportional-hazards / Poisson-process derivation
    of cloglog).

We deduplicate to one row per S1 spec and look at the best AUROC achievable in
each `(family, exposure_mode)` cell.

```{python}
#| label: s1-heatmap
#| fig-cap: "S1 — best `s1_auroc_oos` (out of 16 covariate combinations) per (family × exposure_mode) cell. Higher = better. The `logit / offset` cell is empty by design — `offset` is only valid with `cloglog`."
H.plot_stage_heatmap(s1_df, "s1", "s1_auroc_oos",
                     lower_better=False, log_scale=False,
                     facet_by_threshold=False)
plt.show()
```

**What we saw:**

- `cloglog × free` and `logit × free` are tied at 0.785 AUROC (Brier
  values agree to within ~0.001 as well). Identical ordering of fitted
  probabilities → identical AUROC, and the MLE pins the fitted-probability
  surface to nearly the same values for the two links when the data sits
  in moderate-p range. So the family choice carries no information here.
- `cloglog × offset` collapses to 0.58 AUROC. The offset locks the
  log_exposed slope at 1, which is the wrong empirical slope (we expect
  more-exposed populations to have a *sub*-1 slope on per-person rate),
  so the model is forced into a misspecification it cannot escape.
- `excluded` (no exposure information at all) sits at 0.72 — better than
  `offset` but well below `free`, confirming that exposure information
  carries real signal when used correctly.

::: {.callout-tip title="Decision — S1"}
**`s1_family = logit`, `s1_exposure_mode = free`.**

Drop `cloglog` (identical metrics, less canonical link, harder small-sample
behavior — we'd only keep it for the proportional-hazards interpretation,
which doesn't survive once we move off `offset`). Drop `excluded` and
`offset`.

Uncertainty propagation works identically for both links — both fit via
`sm.GLM`, both expose `cov_params()` for joint-coefficient sampling — so
that is not a discriminator.
:::

## S2 — `P(rate >= threshold | deaths >= 1)`

S2's outcome IS threshold-dependent: the binary label `rate >= threshold`
shifts as `threshold_quantile` moves. We deduplicate per
`(s2_family, s2_exposure_mode, s2_cov, threshold_quantile)` and look at
each threshold separately. Same family/exposure_mode question as S1.

```{python}
#| label: s2-heatmap
#| fig-cap: "S2 — best `s2_auroc_oos` per (family × exposure_mode) cell, faceted by threshold. The free / excluded contrast is the dominant signal at every threshold."
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 6
H.plot_stage_heatmap(s2_df, "s2", "s2_auroc_oos",
                     lower_better=False, log_scale=False,
                     facet_by_threshold=True)
plt.show()
```

**What we saw:**

- `logit` and `cloglog` agree to within ~0.001 AUROC in every comparable
  cell across all thresholds — same identifiability story as S1.
- `free` dominates `excluded` everywhere, with a widening gap at higher
  thresholds (0.92 vs 0.71 at threshold = 0.80; 0.96 vs 0.80 at 0.95).

::: {.callout-tip title="Decision — S2"}
**`s2_family = logit`, `s2_exposure_mode = free`** at every threshold.

Drop `cloglog` and `excluded`. Same reasoning as S1, applied across all
thresholds.
:::

## Bulk — `E[rate | deaths >= 1, rate < threshold]`

Bulk has six candidate families: `beta`, `gamma`, `lognormal`,
`scaled_logit`, `nb`, `poisson`. Three of them (`beta`, `scaled_logit`,
`truncated_normal` — though truncated_normal is bulk-eligible only in
limited form) enforce the bound `predicted_rate < threshold` by
construction. The other rate families (`gamma`, `lognormal`) and the
count families (`nb`, `poisson`) can predict rates above threshold,
extrapolating outside the bulk training support.

Bulk is doubly threshold-dependent: the training set changes with
threshold, *and* absolute-error metrics (`mae_rate`, `rmse_rate`,
`mae_count`) shift in scale across thresholds. So we read MAE/RMSE
*within* a threshold, not across, and use rank-based comparisons for
cross-threshold consistency.

### Family choice

```{python}
#| label: bulk-family-exposure-heatmap
#| fig-cap: "Bulk — best `bulk_mae_rate_oos` per (family × exposure_mode) cell, faceted by threshold. Lower is better. Same-threshold rows are directly comparable; absolute values are not comparable across thresholds. `beta` and `scaled_logit` are tied for best in nearly every threshold."
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 8
H.plot_stage_heatmap(bulk_df, "bulk", "bulk_mae_rate_oos",
                     lower_better=True, log_scale=True,
                     facet_by_threshold=True)
plt.show()
```

**Cross-threshold rank stability.** A natural worry: maybe the right
bulk family at threshold = 0.80 is different from threshold = 0.95.
We check by computing the Kendall τ between the rankings at each pair
of thresholds — high τ means the same configurations win regardless
of threshold.

```{python}
#| label: bulk-kendall
#| fig-cap: "Cross-threshold Kendall τ between configuration rankings, one panel per metric. Adjacent thresholds agree at τ > 0.85 in every metric, and τ ≥ 0.5 even between the most distant thresholds (0.70 vs 0.95). Rankings are stable enough that the family decision transfers across thresholds."
#| fig-width: 18
#| fig-height: 4
BULK_METRICS = [
    "bulk_mae_rate_oos", "bulk_rmse_rate_oos", "bulk_cor_rate_oos",
    "bulk_mae_count_oos", "bulk_cor_count_oos",
]
BULK_DIR = {
    "bulk_mae_rate_oos":  "lower",
    "bulk_rmse_rate_oos": "lower",
    "bulk_cor_rate_oos":  "higher",
    "bulk_mae_count_oos": "lower",
    "bulk_cor_count_oos": "higher",
}
H.kendall_threshold_heatmap(bulk_df, "bulk", BULK_METRICS,
                            metric_directions=BULK_DIR)
plt.show()
```

**What we saw:**

- `scaled_logit` anchors the lowest-MAE cells at every threshold, with
  a clear gap over `beta` that **widens with threshold** (~14% lower MAE
  at q=0.70, ~25% at q=0.85, ~38% at q=0.95). On the all-years data
  these two families were tied; on the post-2000 data scaled_logit
  pulls clear.
- `gamma` and `lognormal` are competitive at low thresholds but drift
  toward higher MAE as threshold rises, consistent with their lack of
  upper-bound enforcement.
- `nb` and `poisson` (the count-bulk families) live in the offset-only
  column and are worse than the rate families at every threshold.
- The Kendall τ matrix shows τ ≥ 0.62 between any pair of adjacent
  thresholds on `bulk_mae_rate_oos` and ≥ 0.66 on `bulk_cor_rate_oos`,
  so the family decision transfers across thresholds.

::: {.callout-tip title="Decision — Bulk family"}
**`bulk_family = scaled_logit`** (single family this cycle, vs the
all-years `{beta, scaled_logit}` pair).

Drop `beta`, `gamma`, `lognormal`, `nb`, `poisson`. Both surviving
bounded-rate families (`beta`, `scaled_logit`) enforce
`predicted_rate ∈ (0, threshold)` by construction, so the structural
argument applies to both — the decision to narrow to one is purely
empirical, driven by the size and consistency of `scaled_logit`'s MAE
advantage on this dataset.
:::

### Exposure mode

Restricting to `scaled_logit`, we re-examine the `exposure_mode` ×
covariate plane. Rate-bulk families have four exposure modes available:
`excluded`, `free`, `free+weight`, `weight`.

```{python}
#| label: bulk-focus-heatmap
#| fig-cap: "Bulk family × exposure_mode heatmap restricted to `scaled_logit`. `free` and `free+weight` dominate at every threshold."
#| fig-width: 14
#| fig-height: 5
bulk_focus_df = bulk_df[bulk_df["bulk_family"].isin(["scaled_logit"])].copy()
H.plot_stage_heatmap(bulk_focus_df, "bulk", "bulk_mae_rate_oos",
                     lower_better=True, log_scale=True,
                     facet_by_threshold=True)
plt.show()
```

**What we saw:**

- `free` is consistently the lowest-MAE cell at every threshold.
- `free+weight` is within ~5-10% of `free` everywhere.
- `excluded` (no exposure term) and `weight` (population weights only,
  no slope on `log_exposed`) are ~25-40% worse at every threshold.
- The pattern matches S1 / S2: there IS exposure information, dropping
  it or fixing its slope hurts. `free` lets the data choose the slope;
  `free+weight` adds population weighting on top.

::: {.callout-tip title="Decision — Bulk exposure mode"}
**`bulk_exposure_mode ∈ {free, free+weight}`**.

Drop `excluded` and `weight`. Both surviving modes are kept open
because the choice between them is downstream — they produce similar
rankings, and the call between weighted and unweighted IRLS is a
calibration question, not a screening one.
:::

## Tail — `E[rate | deaths >= 1, rate >= threshold]`

Tail has more candidate families (eight: `gamma`, `gpd`, `log_logistic`,
`lognormal`, `nb`, `poisson`, `truncated_normal`, `weibull`) and a noisier
ranking story than bulk. The cross-threshold Kendall τ heatmap drops
into the 0.4–0.5 range for adjacent thresholds in count metrics — there
is more shuffling here than in bulk. The cleanest framing for the
screening question is therefore not "what's best on average" but
"which families have *any* covariate / exposure_mode combination that
reaches the top 10 anywhere?"

```{python}
#| label: tail-attainability
#| fig-cap: "Tail family attainability: per family, the *best* rank achieved across covariate × exposure_mode choices, at every (metric × threshold) cell. Green = the family has a config that reaches top 10 in that cell. Red = it doesn't anywhere it appears, regardless of covariate or exposure_mode. Discrete color bins (1–4 / 5–10 / 11–20 / 21–40 / >40) make top-10 attainability a one-look read."
#| fig-width: 16
#| fig-height: 5
TAIL_METRICS = [
    "tail_mae_rate_oos", "tail_rmse_rate_oos", "tail_cor_rate_oos",
    "tail_mae_count_oos", "tail_cor_count_oos",
]
TAIL_DIR = {
    "tail_mae_rate_oos":  "lower",
    "tail_rmse_rate_oos": "lower",
    "tail_cor_rate_oos":  "higher",
    "tail_mae_count_oos": "lower",
    "tail_cor_count_oos": "higher",
}
H.family_attainability_heatmap(tail_df, "tail", TAIL_METRICS,
                               metric_directions=TAIL_DIR,
                               top_n=10, drop_threshold=20)
plt.show()
```

**What we saw:**

- `nb` and `poisson` are uniformly red. Their best-rank-achievable is
  > 20 in nearly every (metric × threshold) cell — and structurally
  they are the wrong tool: count GLMs have no threshold awareness, so
  their predicted rates can land below threshold (a contradiction
  with the conditional eval set `rate >= threshold`).
- `gpd`, `log_logistic`, `lognormal`, `truncated_normal` reach top-10
  in nearly every cell. They are the families that fit on
  `excess_rate = rate - threshold` (or, for `truncated_normal`, on
  log-rate with a lower truncation at log(threshold)) — both
  formulations enforce `predicted_rate >= threshold` by construction.
- `gamma` is mixed: rank 2–4 on rate metrics at low thresholds but
  rank 32–45 in MAE-count cells. Ceiling is high but the failure mode
  is broad enough to flag.
- `weibull` never breaks top 10 in some cells but never goes truly bad
  (worst best-rank is 16). On the bubble.

::: {.callout-tip title="Decision — Tail"}
**Drop `nb` and `poisson`.** Tentatively keep `gpd`, `log_logistic`,
`lognormal`, `truncated_normal`, `weibull`, `gamma` — but flag
**`gamma` and `weibull` as suspicious** pending closer look in the next
iteration.

Same structural argument as bulk: the families that enforce the
conditional support (`predicted_rate >= threshold`) by construction
are precisely the ones that cluster at the top of the rankings.
:::

## Threshold — deferred

Threshold selection is **not** locked in this report. Per §Scope, the
threshold question is best answered on storm-level rather than config-level
data, which is computed in the intermediate-survivors stage. The
preliminary screen above culls the 73,054 calibrated → 583 surviving DH
configurations across all six thresholds (0.70 through 0.95). Those 583
survivors carry their threshold attribute through to the intermediate
notebook, where:

- Per-storm predicted vs observed scatter plots inform which thresholds
  produce models that actually track storm-level severity.
- A **drop-top-N** trimmed `fwd_pred_obs_ratio` filter (see DECISIONS
  2026-05-17 — "Calibration metric for Nargis-dominated data") removes the
  mega-event-domination effect and exposes bulk-of-storms calibration.
- Coverage trajectories on the per-storm pool replace the
  `full_coverage_rate_L_oos` panel.

The threshold decision then falls out of the storm-level evidence, not from
config-level summary statistics on a Nargis-dominated aggregate.

## Decision summary

| Stage     | Family kept                                                                | Exposure mode kept    | Notes                                                                  |
|-----------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| S1        | `logit`                                                                    | `free`                | logit & cloglog identical (~0.795 AUROC); offset misspecified         |
| S2        | `logit`                                                                    | `free`                | log_exposed alone explains most discrimination; gap widens with threshold |
| Bulk      | `scaled_logit`                                                             | `free`, `free+weight` | beta dropped this cycle — scaled_logit wins clearly on post-2000 data  |
| Tail      | `gpd`, `log_logistic`, `lognormal`, `truncated_normal`, `gamma`, `weibull` | open                  | drop `nb` and `poisson`; gamma / weibull / truncated_normal suspicious |
| Threshold | **deferred**                                                               | —                     | Decided in intermediate-survivors notebook with per-storm predictions  |

Each entry above is logged as a discrete decision in `.claude/DECISIONS.md`
with revisit conditions. The asymmetric calibration gate
(`full_pred_obs_ratio_oos ∈ [0.1, 1.5]`) is logged as a 2026-05-17
decision; the bulk-family narrowing to `scaled_logit` is a deliberate
divergence from the all-years cycle and is logged as such.
